title: Protective Eyewear Selection Assessment
description: Test your ability to calculate OD requirements and select appropriate laser protective eyewear

questions.question: What does OD stand for in laser safety?
questions.correct_feedback: Correct! OD stands for Optical Density, which measures how much a filter attenuates laser light.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Incorrect. OD stands for Optical Density - it measures the attenuation of light passing through a filter.

questions.question: If OD = 3, what percentage of light is transmitted through the filter?
Calculate the required OD for a laser exposure where E_actual = 1000 mW/cm² and MPE = 1 mW/cm².
questions.incorrect_feedback: Use OD = log₁₀(1000/1) = log₁₀(1000). Remember that log₁₀(1000) = 3.

questions.question: A 500 mW, 532 nm laser has a beam diameter of 2 mm at the eye. Calculate the irradiance.
questions.hint: Convert mm to cm before calculating area.
questions.correct_feedback: Correct! Area = π × (0.1 cm)² = 0.0314 cm². E = 500 / 0.0314 = 15,915 mW/cm².
questions.incorrect_feedback: Convert diameter: 2 mm = 0.2 cm, radius = 0.1 cm. Area = π × (0.1)² = 0.0314 cm². Then E = 500/0.0314.

questions.question: A laser safety officer calculates required OD = 4.2. What OD eyewear should be selected?
questions.correct_feedback: Correct! Always round UP to the next integer OD for safety margin. OD 4.2 → OD 5.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Incorrect. Always round UP to the next standard OD level to provide safety margin. OD 4.2 rounds up to OD 5.

questions.question: Which statement about eyewear VLT (Visible Light Transmission) is TRUE?

options.text: Higher VLT always means better protection
VLT and OD are inversely related
VLT must be above 50% for safety

questions.correct_feedback: Correct! As OD increases, VLT decreases. Higher protection means less visible light transmission.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Incorrect. VLT (Visible Light Transmission) and OD are inversely related - higher OD means lower VLT.

questions.question: For alignment tasks where the user needs to see the beam spot, what type of eyewear is most appropriate?
questions.correct_feedback: Correct! Alignment eyewear with lower OD (2-4) allows visibility of the beam while providing protection for the alignment task.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Incorrect. Alignment eyewear with lower OD (2-4) is specifically designed for tasks requiring beam visibility.

questions.question: Calculate the required OD for a 10 W, 1064 nm Nd:YAG laser with 5 mm beam diameter at the eye (0.25 s exposure). Assume CA = 5.0 for 1064 nm.
questions.hint: MPE = 5 × 2.56 mW/cm² = 12.8 mW/cm². Calculate irradiance, then ratio, then OD.
questions.correct_feedback: Correct! E = 10,000/(π×0.25²) = 50,930 mW/cm². MPE = 12.8 mW/cm². Ratio = 3,979. OD = log₁₀(3,979) = 3.6 → round to 4.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Calculate irradiance: 10,000 mW / (π × 0.25² cm²) = 50,930 mW/cm². MPE with CA=5: 5 × 2.56 = 12.8 mW/cm². Ratio = 3,979. OD = log(3,979) ≈ 3.6 → OD 4.

questions.question: A lab has both 532 nm and 1064 nm lasers. Which eyewear strategy is generally NOT recommended?

options.text: Two separate pairs of narrowband eyewear
532 nm eyewear only (since it has lower MPE)

questions.correct_feedback: Correct! Using 532 nm eyewear only would leave eyes unprotected at 1064 nm - a dangerous practice.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Incorrect. Using only 532 nm eyewear would not protect against 1064 nm radiation, creating a serious hazard.

questions.question: An eyewear specification shows 'D LB7' marking. What does this indicate?

options.text: OD 7 for continuous wave lasers with large beam diameter

questions.correct_feedback: Correct! 'D' indicates continuous wave testing, 'LB' indicates large beam, '7' is the OD rating.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Incorrect. Per EN 207: 'D' = continuous wave (>0.5s), 'LB' = large beam test condition, '7' = OD 7+.
